Under IBM, Confluent’s financial, legal, and HR teams can be absorbed into IBM’s existing teams, especially since its large scale allows it to process these functions at a lower cost than Confluent.

As such, I’m estimating G&A costs to represent 10% of Confluent’s revenues in the first year post deal, improving to 6% in the fifth year post deal.

Assuming a 20% tax rate and considering IBM’s diluted share count of 948.9 million, my 5-year model estimates Confluent to start contributing positively to IBM’s EPS in the second year post deal, while generating a 26% EBIT margin and contributing $0.77 to IBM’s EPS in the fifth year post deal. These estimates are consistent with IBM’s M&A rationale, shared in its 2025 Investor Day presentation, targeting a 20% PTI margin in the fifth year post acquisitions.

Risks

The main risk to my bullish thesis on both Confluent and IBM is the acquisition deal getting delayed or blocked by regulators. For Confluent, any delay beyond mid-2026 would negatively impact the arbitrage premium which may not make it as attractive as it currently is, while the deal getting blocked could see the stock plummet near its pre-acquisition levels.

As for IBM, the deal getting blocked would represent a massive blow to its software segment's potential growth due to Confluent’s ability to improve its offerings’ value proposition and growing its topline through upselling and cross-selling Confluent’s data streaming platform to existing enterprise customers.

IBM also faces risks of integrating Confluent into its operations which could delay the expected cost synergies, and thus, delaying Confluent’s positive contribution to IBM’s EPS. Finally, IBM could face risks while optimizing Confluent’s SG&A costs either due to IBM consulting failing to integrate Confluent into its sales funnel or excessive cost cuts that could hinder Confluent’s innovation pace and developer relationships which represent the core of its value.

Conclusion

In summary, Confluent presents an attractive opportunity for arbitrageurs with its current arbitrage premium over the 6-month treasury, especially with the fed’s latest rate cut, given the high likelihood of the IBM acquisition going through. As for IBM, acquiring Confluent could rapidly accelerate its growth thanks to the growing demand for data streaming and IBM’s potential to unlock Confluent’s true value with its go-to-market scale. Moreover, Confluent could help improve the value proposition of IBM’s software stack with its streaming capabilities, potentially driving demand for watsonx’s platform.

From a financial perspective, combining Confluent’s value proposition with IBM’s scale, high penetration of Fortune 500 enterprises, and global presence creates opportunities to drive high-value customers to Confluent’s platform and expand their consumption of its products. At the same time, the potential SG&A synergies could see Confluent start contributing positively to IBM’s EPS starting from the second year post deal, while reaching a 26% EBIT margin and a 20.8% profit margin in the fifth year post deal.

data.title: XFLT: We May Not Have Found The Bottom Yet In This High Yield CEF
data.description:
In my continuing search for income investments that are appropriate for retirees or someone approaching retirement, I am constantly on the lookout for opportunities to take advantage of tactical buying situations in high yield income funds. Back in April 2024, I suggested funding your retirement with an income fund that yielded 14% at the time and a discounted DRIP (dividend reinvestment policy) program that could potentially add to the income generating potential of an investment in the fund known as the XAI Octagon Floating Rate & Alternative Income Trust (XFLT).

That article turned out to be not one of my better calls in the past couple of years. A few months after that article was published, the Fed started to lower interest rates (beginning in September 2024) and XFLT was forced to cut the monthly distribution in December 2024. Then, in June of this year, XFLT cut the distribution again, this time from $0.077 to $0.07.

Furthermore, the NAV declined from a 52-week high of $6.70 a year ago to just $5.43 as of 12/9/25. The market has punished the stock even further by sending the market price to a near 52-week low discount of almost -15% versus a slight premium to NAV a year ago as shown on CEFConnect.

As a result, the one-year total return of XFLT is a miserable -20%. A very disappointing result for investors who bought into the fund a year ago. However, XFLT may have found a bottom and could be a potential winner going forward. For that reason, I rate XFLT a Hold as we approach the end of 2025. However, additional interest rate cuts could lead to another distribution cut in 2026, and if that happens a further decline in the market price is likely.

As Kim Flynn, Partner and President of XFLT stated on the December Q3 2025 earnings call, lower interest rates have also been a benefit to the fund in terms of lower leverage costs:

Now as rates have come down, we've benefited from lower leverage expenses because our leverage -- a large part of our leverage is floating rate. And so we wanted to highlight that it's been a period where there's been a lot of change in the year ended 9/30. We're remaining steady with our monthly distributions, our most recent 3 monthly distribution declarations were at $0.07 per share, and we continue to manage distributions as we have in the past.

However, another CEF that is similar to XFLT in its holdings that includes mostly CLO debt (while XFLT is about 50/50 CLOs and senior loans) is Eagle Point Income fund (EIC). I last wrote about EIC in November 2023 when interest rates were still on the rise and at that time I suggested buying the fund that had delivered 15% annual returns and was still soaring.

However, in the last year EIC has also delivered a dismal total return of -20% and has cut the dividend dramatically, from $0.20 per share to $0.13 in July, and then again to $0.11 as of January 2026. I suspect that XFLT may also be forced to cut its distribution again in 2026.

XFLT Overview and Review of Holdings

When I covered XFLT back in August 2023, I wrote about how a fund like XFLT with its policy to DRIP (reinvest shares) at a discount to NAV could enhance your income stream, helping to generate a river of cash in retirement. At that time, XFLT had just increased the dividend.

Since I last wrote about XFLT, the fund managers announced an increase in the distribution in May of this year by 16%, from a previous dividend of $0.07 to $0.085 monthly, now yielding 15% annually. And if you elect to participate in the DRIP and your broker (I use Fidelity) supports the discounted DRIP, you can benefit from an additional increase of up to 5% per month by reinvesting those dividends rather than taking them as cash.

A snapshot of the fund from the website shows that the current NAV as of 12/9/25 (unlike other CLO funds, XFLT updates NAV daily) was $5.43. The closing price of $4.62 represents a discount of about -15% and a distribution yield of 18%. Leverage of 38.66% is on the high side and contributes to the downside return dynamics, however, as stated on the earnings call the fund managers are working to lower leverage costs as rates come down.

The asset allocation as of 9/30/25 as shown on the fund website is generally 50/50, weighted almost equally between CLOs and senior loans, with senior secured first lien loans at about 46% of the total portfolio value, followed by 36.8% in CLO Equity and 13.5% in CLO Debt.

The CLO Equity allocation has mostly contributed to the NAV decline over the past few months. Nearly all CLO Equity funds including OXLC, ECC, OCCI, CCIF, and SPMC have also seen rapid declines in their NAV due to compressed spreads and lower valuations for the equity tranches of CLOs.

On the December XFLT earnings call, Senior Portfolio Manager Lauren Law addressed that very important topic of the decline in NAV.

XFLT invest in broadly syndicated leveraged loans directly, CLO BB mezzanine tranches and of course, CLO equity. The loan market in total performed well in the third quarter. XFLT's loan allocation did as well. We actually did better than the market. Single B rated loans were the best performing portion of the loan market and XFLT is significantly overweight that rating cohort. The same was true for BB CLO tranches and the trust holdings of them.

CLO equity on the other hand, as I mentioned, had another tough quarter of performance in Q3. And while there's no index to quote CLO equity returns, many sell-side observers do post estimates. As far as we can tell, the trust equity performance did exceed the estimated performance of the market by a fairly good degree as well. However, it was a challenging period for CLO equity and the trust equity allocation was a drag on fund total return relative to its loan and BB mezz.

Regarding the tightened spreads, which affect the income generation of the XFLT portfolio, Ms. Law also addressed that topic with some helpful detail. This slide from the Q3 2025 investor presentation shows the tightening spread in 2025 that widened briefly after the April tariff tantrum.

Yes, spread tightening has been a continued challenge for the market this year. To say it very simply as to how this impacts XFLT, spread tightening does pressure the income earned by the trust. It pressures the income we earn on the loans and it pressures the income we earn on CLO equity. Tight and tightening spreads have been a continuing theme. They have weighed on CLO equity performance. Broadly syndicated leveraged loans benefit from 6 months of call protection. And that just means a borrower cannot reduce their interest rate in the first 6 months of the life of a loan. CLO liabilities, on the other hand, can only be refinanced lower every 2 years as they are structured with 2 years of call protection.

When asked if the market is near a floor in terms of CLO equity arbitrage and loan spreads, Ms. Law was optimistic in her comments regarding the outlook for 2026. Current spreads are at historically tight levels not seen since pre-2020 largely due to ongoing market uncertainty leading to a period of “technical imbalance.” An increase in M&A activity is expected to occur in Q1 2026 which should help to stimulate new loan issuance leading to wider spreads.

The forward calendar contains the largest ever LBO announced. That will be launching into the market very early in Q1. There are other large announced transactions that will follow it, and all signs point to increased M&A activity in addition to what is already announced. And so all of that is to say that I think spreads, while they may tick lower before they move higher I think, are very, very close to a floor and that should lead to a situation where CLO equity arbitrage is not going to get worse from here.

Looking at a longer-term view of XFLT we can see that the fund is approaching its all-time low NAV (when it hit $4.49 in March 2020) and now trades at a historically low discount to NAV of about -15%. However, given the fact that the economy is not currently in a recession, nor is there significant fear around credit risk other than the recent “cockroaches in the coal mine” that Howard Marks referred to in his recent memo, my prediction is that NAV will begin to stabilize near the current level and the price will begin to move back up as the discount closes.

The only time since inception that XFLT has traded at a lower discount was briefly in December 2018, and then in March 2020, and it quickly recovered afterward each time. Will this time be different?

In their Q3 2025 investor presentation, the company includes a slide showing previous CLO BB Debt Drawdowns and the recovery time for each. This slide does not include the latest one that occurred in Q4 (and which we are still experiencing) because it was based on results through 9/30/25, but it does offer encouraging insights based on recent history.

When asked about managing the risk of CLO equity in the fund’s portfolio and the outlook for 2026, Lauren Law explained that the two important factors they can control include 1) resets and refinancing activity, and 2) selection of active, best-in-class CLO managers.

The one main driver of CLO equity returns is the value of the cash flow stream earned by the equity. And as discussed, this has been under pressure as the income on loans has come down, spreads have repriced more quickly than CLO liabilities. And so to manage that element of return, we reset and refinance actively, which means we are targeting the equity we buy to be able to generate value from this reset and refinancing activity and then we push for that activity to occur as quickly as possible upon the expiration of non-call periods.

So a lot of our management of our equity book has resulted around picking managers, who actively manage credit risk and actively manage it successfully. Our views of managers can change over time. We are constantly analyzing recent performance, extended periods of performance and trying to anticipate which managers are going to continue to perform and which may not and trading our book accordingly.

Conclusion: Further Rate Cuts Could Cause Continued CLO Equity Pressure

Although they danced around this issue to some degree, preferring to focus on the good points in the XFLT fund portfolio that involve strong cash flows from senior loans and positive CLO Debt fundamentals, the elephant in the room remains CLO Equity.

Now that the Fed has announced yet another rate cut for 2025, loan spreads remain tight and may continue to put pressure on the CLO Equity tranches in the coming months unless we see a sudden pickup in new loan issuance associated with an increase in M&A activity in Q1 2026. That scenario does appear to be what the XFLT portfolio managers are counting on and if that is what happens then we could indeed start to see some improvement in CLO Equity assets.

With my mildly optimistic view of the market outlook for 2026, I do believe that we may have seen the bottom in the NAV of XFLT, however, the market price could remain discounted for quite some time until investors are convinced that a real recovery in CLO Equity has begun.
